metadata.seed: Well, I feel really confident in that moment. It feels very real. Like any moment they're going to kick me out. I, like, I'm just not doing well.

patient.query: Well, I feel really confident in that moment. It feels very real. Like any moment they're going to kick me out. I, like, I'm just not doing well.

automatic_thoughts: they're going to kick me out

llm_response.response: It sounds like you're experiencing a mix of confidence and worry about being let go. What evidence supports the idea that they'll actually kick you out? And what evidence supports the feeling that you're doing well?

patient.query: I don’t know... I keep thinking about the times my boss has made comments that felt like they were hinting I’m not good enough. But then again, I get positive feedback from my coworkers, and sometimes I get compliments on my work. It’s just hard to focus on the good stuff when those negative thoughts creep in.

automatic_thoughts: I should focus on the negative

llm_response.response: It sounds like those comments have been hard to shake, even when you receive praise. What evidence do you have that those comments mean you’re not good enough? Are there other ways you might interpret what your boss was trying to convey?

patient.query: Yeah, it really does stick with me. I guess I just take things like that to heart, you know? I think it’s because I’ve always struggled with feeling like I’m not meeting expectations, so when my boss says something... it feels like they’re confirming that fear. But then, maybe they just have high standards, and I’m too hard on myself. It’s confusing.

neutral: Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)
Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re noticing a pattern where your boss’s comments feel like a confirmation of your fear of not meeting expectations. What evidence do you have that the boss’s words are actually a reflection of your performance, and what evidence might suggest they’re just setting high standards?

patient.query: I mean, I guess there are times when I’ve done well on projects, and I’ve gotten praise from my team, which feels good. But then, it’s like when my boss critiques me, I focus on that instead of the positives. I don’t know, it’s just easier to believe the negative stuff. I wish I could just let those comments roll off my back, but they linger... like I’m always waiting for the next piece of feedback that’ll tear me down.

entailment: Tends to be critical of others (finding)
neutral: Tends to be sensitive to criticism (finding)
contradiction: Tends to appear indifferent to praise or criticism (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like the negative comments feel heavier than the praise you receive. What evidence do you have that the criticism matters more than the positive feedback? How might you give yourself the same kindness you offer others when they speak kindly to you?

patient.query: Yeah, it does feel heavier, like it weighs me down. I guess it’s because I’ve always been told that you have to learn from criticism to improve, so I focus on that. But I don’t really think about how I’d react if a friend came to me with a similar situation. I’d probably tell them to focus on the good and not let one comment define them. It’s just so much harder to do that for myself.

automatic_thoughts: I have to learn from criticism to improve
I can't react positively for myself

neutral: Tends to be sensitive to criticism (finding)
Fear of being a burden to others (finding)
Tends to be critical of others (finding)
Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like the idea that you must learn from criticism is weighing on you. What evidence do you have that this focus is the only path to improvement, and how might you notice moments when you’re already doing well? If you could treat yourself with the same kindness you’d offer a friend, what might that look like in this situation?

patient.query: It feels heavy, like it weighs me down. I focus on the criticism because I’ve always been told that it’s how you learn and improve. But I don’t think about how I’d react if a friend came to me with a similar situation. I’d probably tell them to focus on the good and not let one comment define them, but I just struggle to do that for myself.

automatic_thoughts: It's how you learn and improve
I’d probably tell them to focus on the good

neutral: Tends to be sensitive to criticism (finding)
Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like the criticism feels like a heavy weight on you. What evidence do you have that one comment really defines you? How might you view the situation differently if you were talking to a friend?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: automatic thought: one comment defines me
retrieved_concepts_used.label: automatic_thoughts

patient.query: I don’t know... it just feels so real in the moment, you know? Like when they say something is off, it makes me question everything I did. I guess if I were talking to a friend, I’d remind them that one person’s opinion doesn’t define their worth. But when it comes to me, it’s like all that good stuff I’ve done just fades away, and I feel like I’m back to square one. It’s frustrating.

automatic_thoughts: one person's opinion defines my worth
all the good stuff I've done fades away

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: I hear how that comment made you feel like everything you did is suddenly in doubt. What evidence do you have that one person’s opinion can erase all the good you’ve done? Can you think of moments when you felt proud of yourself despite criticism?

patient.query: Yeah, I mean... I did have that project last month that went really well, and my team was proud of it. I felt really good about that. But when I think about the feedback from my boss, it’s like all those moments just get overshadowed. I guess I can see that those proud moments are still there, but it’s hard to hold onto them when I feel so weighed down by negative comments. It’s like they just scream louder in my head.

automatic_thoughts: all those moments just get overshadowed
it's hard to hold onto proud moments
they just scream louder in my head

llm_response.response: It sounds like the negative feedback feels louder than the proud moments. What evidence do you have that the negative comments are more important than the positive ones?

patient.query: I guess I don’t really have solid evidence... it’s just how it feels to me. The negative comments seem to stick, like they replay in my head over and over. The praise feels fleeting—like, sure, it’s nice in the moment, but those criticisms seem to linger forever. It’s almost like I have this instinct to protect myself from feeling hurt again, so I just focus on the things that bring me down, even though I know that's not fair to myself.

automatic_thoughts: the negative comments replay in my head
I focus on the things that bring me down

entailment: Tends to be sensitive to criticism (finding)
neutral: Tends to be critical of others (finding)
contradiction: Tends to appear indifferent to praise or criticism (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like the negative comments feel like a persistent echo, while the praise fades quickly, leaving you feeling more hurt than uplifted. That instinct to guard yourself by focusing on the downsides is a common way people try to protect against further pain. Recognizing that this pattern isn’t fair to yourself is an important first step toward easing that cycle.

patient.query: It’s just how it feels to me. The negative comments stick around, replaying in my head. The praise feels fleeting, nice in the moment, but the criticisms linger forever. It’s like I instinctively focus on the things that bring me down, even though I know it’s not fair to myself. I guess I just wish I could hold on to the good feelings longer.

automatic_thoughts: focus on things that bring me down
wish I could hold on to good feelings longer

neutral: Tends to be sensitive to criticism (finding)
contradiction: Tends to appear indifferent to praise or criticism (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like the negative feedback feels like it has a longer hold on you than the positive. What evidence do you have that the praise doesn’t stay with you at all? Can you recall any moments when a compliment or a kind word lingered in your mind for a while?
